section.title: Final Instructions and Greetings
section.sectionRationale: Paul closes the letter with rapid, practical instructions for the life of the church: honor and love those who labor and lead, live at peace, care for the disorderly, the fainthearted, and the weak, never repay evil, and in every circumstance rejoice, pray, and give thanks. He tells them not to quench the Spirit but to test everything and hold the good. Then he grounds it all in a prayer, that the God of peace would Himself sanctify them wholly and keep them blameless until Christ comes, for He who calls them is faithful and will do it.
section.thesis: The shared life of the church is marked by honor for faithful leaders, patient love for one another, and constant joy, prayer, and thanksgiving that keep the Spirit's fire burning; and its confidence rests not on human effort but on the faithful God who calls His people and will Himself sanctify and keep them blameless until the coming of the Lord Jesus.
section.frameNote: Three movements: love and peace within the body, honoring those who lead and bearing with one another; the marks of a Spirit-filled life, rejoicing, praying, giving thanks, and testing all things; and the closing prayer and blessing, that the faithful God who calls will sanctify and keep His people blameless until Christ comes.

units.label: Love and peace within the body
units.note: honor leaders, bear with one another

verses.text: But we ask you, brothers, to acknowledge those who work diligently among you, who care for you in the Lord and who {{noutheteo|admonish}} you.
In love, hold them in {{hegeomai|highest regard}} because of their work. Live in peace with one another.
And we urge you, brothers, to admonish the {{ataktos|unruly}}, encourage the {{oligopsychos|fainthearted}}, help the weak, and be patient with everyone.
Make sure that no one repays evil for evil. Always pursue what is good for one another and for all people.

units.label: The marks of a Spirit-filled life
units.note: rejoice, pray, give thanks, test all things

verses.text: {{eucharisteo|Give thanks}} in every circumstance, for this is God’s will for you in Christ Jesus.
but {{dokimazo|test}} all things. Hold fast to what is good.
Abstain from every form of evil.

units.label: The faithful God will keep you
units.note: sanctified wholly, kept blameless, by the One who calls

verses.text: Now may the God of peace Himself {{hagiazo|sanctify}} you {{holoteles|completely}}, and may your entire spirit, soul, and body be kept {{amemptos|blameless}} at the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ.
The One who {{kaleo|calls}} you is {{pistos|faithful}}, and He will do it.
Brothers, pray for us as well.
Greet all the brothers with a holy kiss.
I charge you before the Lord to have this letter read to all the brothers.
The {{charis|grace}} of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you.

noutheteo.gloss: To admonish, warn, or counsel by putting truth into someone's mind. It describes the loving correction that leaders give and that the whole church is to give the unruly. Far from harshness, it is the caring instruction of those who watch over souls and want to see one another kept from harm.

hegeomai.gloss: To consider, esteem, or hold in regard. Paul tells the church to hold their leaders in the highest regard in love, because of their work. The esteem is not for status but for faithful labor, and it is bound together with love, so that respect for leaders and warmth toward them go hand in hand.

ataktos.gloss: Disorderly, unruly, out of rank, a military term for a soldier who breaks step. It describes those in the church living undisciplined or idle lives, out of line with the gospel. They are to be admonished, corrected in love, not abandoned, so that they may return to ordered, faithful living.

oligopsychos.gloss: Fainthearted, small-souled, discouraged. It pictures those whose spirits have grown faint under pressure or fear. Rather than being pushed, they are to be encouraged, come alongside and strengthened, so that the weary and downcast are lifted rather than left behind.

chairo.gloss: To rejoice, to be glad. Paul commands joy at all times, a settled gladness in God that does not depend on circumstances. This joy is not forced cheerfulness but the fruit of belonging to the Lord, possible even in affliction because its source is Christ and not changing conditions.

adialeiptos.gloss: Without ceasing, constantly, without interruption. Prayer is to be unceasing, not meaning endless words but a continual, God-ward turning of the heart that runs through all of life. It describes a life kept in constant communion with God rather than prayer confined to set moments.

eucharisteo.gloss: To give thanks, to be grateful. Believers are to give thanks in every circumstance, for this is God's will for them in Christ Jesus. Thanksgiving is not for every circumstance as though all were good, but in every circumstance, trusting the God who works all things for the good of His people.

sbennymi.gloss: To quench or extinguish, as one puts out a fire. The Spirit's work is pictured as flame, and the church is warned not to smother it. To quench the Spirit is to resist or stifle His work, whether by despising His gifts or by choking off the life He gives; instead His fire is to be kept burning.

propheteia.gloss: Prophecy, a Spirit-prompted word for the building up of the church. Paul warns against treating such words with contempt, yet in the same breath calls for testing. Prophecy is neither to be despised nor swallowed uncritically; it is to be weighed against the truth and received only where it proves good.

dokimazo.gloss: To test, examine, and prove, as metal is tested for genuineness. Believers are to test all things, weighing every claim and teaching, then holding fast to what is good and rejecting what is evil. Discernment, not gullibility and not cynicism, is the mark of a Spirit-filled and mature church.

hagiazo.gloss: To sanctify, to make holy, to set apart wholly for God. Paul prays that the God of peace Himself would sanctify the believers. Holiness is here God's own work in His people, and the prayer looks for it to be complete, reaching the whole person and bringing them to the day of Christ set apart for Him.

holoteles.gloss: Wholly, completely, through and through, reaching every part to the very end. Paul prays that God would sanctify them completely, and then that their entire spirit, soul, and body be kept. The word stresses that God's sanctifying work leaves nothing out; it claims the whole person for holiness.

amemptos.gloss: Blamelessly, without fault or reproach. The prayer is that the whole person would be kept blameless at the coming of the Lord Jesus. This is not sinless perfection achieved now but the settled goal of God's keeping, a people preserved and made ready to stand unashamed before Christ on that day.

kaleo.gloss: To call, to summon effectively into fellowship with God. The One who calls is God, whose call is not a mere invitation but a powerful summons that creates what it commands. Because the calling is God's own work, the security of the believer rests on His faithfulness to complete what He has begun.

pistos.gloss: Faithful, trustworthy, reliable. The One who calls His people is faithful, and He will do it. The whole weight of the closing prayer rests here: sanctification and preservation are certain not because believers are strong but because God is faithful to His promise and will surely bring His work to completion.

charis.gloss: Grace, God's unearned favor and kindness in Christ. Paul ends as he began, with grace, praying that the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ would be with them. The letter opens and closes in grace, framing every command and every hope within the free favor of God given through His Son.

basic.context: Paul brings the letter to a close with a burst of short, practical commands for church life. He asks them to honor and love the leaders who work hard among them, to live in peace, and to care for the different needs of the disorderly, the fainthearted, and the weak, never repaying evil but always pursuing good. Then come the well-known calls to rejoice always, pray without ceasing, and give thanks in everything, along with the warning not to quench the Spirit but to test all things and hold the good. He seals it with a prayer: that the God of peace would sanctify them completely and keep them blameless until Christ comes, because the God who calls them is faithful and will do it.
basic.hermeneutics: These are commands for ordinary Christian life together, not a random list but the shape of a healthy church. Yet Paul does not leave them as bare duties. He ends by praying that God Himself would accomplish this holiness, and he grounds the whole thing in God's faithfulness. So the passage teaches how believers are to live while resting on the God who works that life in them.
basic.originalLanguages: The church is to noutheteo, admonish, the ataktos, unruly, encourage the oligopsychos, fainthearted, and hold leaders in hegeomai, high regard. They are to chairo, rejoice, always, pray adialeiptos, without ceasing, and eucharisteo, give thanks, in everything. They must not sbennymi, quench, the Spirit or despise propheteia, prophecy, but dokimazo, test, all things. Paul prays that God would hagiazo, sanctify, them holoteles, completely, and keep them amemptos, blameless, for the One who kaleo, calls, them is pistos, faithful. He ends with charis, grace.
basic.typology: The prayer that God would sanctify and keep His people takes up the Old Testament longing for a holy people fit to dwell with a holy God, now answered as God Himself makes them holy in Christ. The unquenched Spirit recalls the fire that was never to go out on the altar, kept burning as a sign of God's abiding presence among His people.
basic.christ: Every command here is life in Christ Jesus, and the whole passage is bracketed by His grace. It is at His coming that the church will be found blameless, and it is His grace with which the letter ends. The holiness Paul prays for is not self-made but the gift of the God of peace, secured by the faithful One who calls, and it is aimed at the day when believers will stand unashamed before the Lord Jesus.
basic.god: God is the God of peace who sanctifies His people, and He is the faithful One who calls them and will surely complete His work. The confidence of the passage rests on His character: He does not merely command holiness, He accomplishes it. Because the God who calls is faithful, the believer's final blamelessness is as certain as God's own reliability.
basic.crossReferences: Philippians 4 also calls believers to rejoice always and to pray with thanksgiving. Philippians 1 promises that He who began a good work will bring it to completion. And Hebrews 13 speaks of the God of peace equipping His people to do His will through Jesus.
basic.oneStory: God has always been working toward a holy people who can dwell with Him. Here at the letter's close, Paul prays that the God of peace would finish that work, sanctifying His people completely and keeping them blameless until Christ appears. The commands describe the life of that people now, and the promise assures them that the faithful God who called them will carry them all the way to the day of the Lord.

context.literary: This is the closing section of the letter, moving from teaching to a cluster of brief exhortations, then to a solemn prayer, personal requests, and a final blessing. The short, staccato commands gather up the practical concerns of the whole letter, and the concluding prayer lifts them from duty to dependence, entrusting the church's holiness to God. The letter ends, as it began, with grace.
context.historical: The Thessalonian church was young, under pressure, and working out how to live together as a body. Some were idle or disorderly, others fainthearted, and there were leaders to be honored and a fellowship to be kept at peace. Paul's rapid instructions address the real texture of their common life, and his charge to have the letter read to all shows he intends it for the whole gathered congregation.
context.canonical: The trio of rejoice, pray, and give thanks has become among the best known of Paul's commands, echoed in his other letters. The prayer for complete sanctification and preservation stands with the New Testament's assurance that God finishes what He begins in His people. The mention of spirit, soul, and body has featured in discussions of human nature, though Paul's point is the wholeness of God's claim on the person.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To shape the common life of the church in love, order, and Spirit-filled devotion, and to assure the believers that the holiness he commands will be accomplished by the faithful God who calls them and will keep them blameless until Christ comes.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This section is largely prescriptive, a series of commands for how the church is to live. But it turns at the end to promise: the same holiness that is commanded is prayed for and guaranteed, because the God who calls is faithful and will do it. Duty and grace are held together.

debates.question: What does it mean to not quench the Spirit and to test prophecies?

views.label: Welcome the Spirit's work, but weigh it
views.case: Believers are neither to smother the Spirit's gifts nor to accept every claimed word uncritically; they test all things against the truth and hold fast to what is good, honoring the Spirit while exercising discernment.

views.label: A caution mainly against suppression
views.case: The stress falls on not stifling genuine works of the Spirit; the testing serves that aim, guarding the church from both deadness and deception.

debates.bearing: Either way the church is called to a discerning openness, valuing the Spirit's work while measuring everything by what is good and true.

debates.question: Does spirit, soul, and body teach three parts of a person?

views.label: A stress on the whole person
views.case: Paul piles up terms to pray that God would sanctify the person completely, leaving no part untouched; the phrase underlines wholeness rather than mapping a precise anatomy of human nature.

views.label: A distinction of parts
views.case: Some read spirit, soul, and body as naming distinct components of human nature, though even then the point serves the prayer for total sanctification.

debates.bearing: The shared point is that God's sanctifying and keeping work claims the entire person, body and inner life alike, for the day of Christ.

study.originalLanguages: Paul asks the church to hold its hardworking leaders in hegeomai, highest regard, in love, and to noutheteo, admonish, the ataktos, the disorderly, while encouraging the oligopsychos, the fainthearted, and helping the weak. Then come the swift commands: chairo, rejoice, at all times; pray adialeiptos, without ceasing; and eucharisteo, give thanks, in every circumstance, for this is God's will in Christ Jesus. They must not sbennymi, quench, the Spirit, nor treat propheteia, prophecy, with contempt, but dokimazo, test, all things and hold fast the good. The letter climbs to its prayer: may the God of peace hagiazo, sanctify, them holoteles, completely, and keep their whole person amemptos, blameless, at the coming of the Lord. The ground of that hope is stated plainly: the One who kaleo, calls, them is pistos, faithful, and He will do it. Paul ends, as he began, with charis, grace.

typology.type: A holy people fit to dwell with a holy God
typology.fulfillment: Israel's calling to be holy is fulfilled as the God of peace Himself sanctifies His people completely in Christ.

typology.type: The fire kept burning on the altar
typology.fulfillment: The command not to quench the Spirit answers the never-extinguished flame, now the abiding fire of the Spirit among God's people.

typology.type: The faithful God who keeps His covenant
typology.fulfillment: God's covenant faithfulness is fulfilled in the One who calls and will surely keep His people blameless to the end.

study.christ: The whole life Paul commands is lived in Christ Jesus, for this is God's will for you in Christ Jesus, and the letter closes with the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ. The goal of the closing prayer is the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ, when His people are to be found blameless. The holiness prayed for is not a human achievement offered to Christ but the fruit of His grace, worked by the God of peace and secured by His faithfulness, so that the church may stand unashamed before the returning Lord. Christ is the beginning, the atmosphere, and the end of this obedient life.
study.god: God dominates the climax of the passage. He is the God of peace who Himself sanctifies, the one who keeps His people whole and blameless, and the faithful One who calls and will do it. The commands are many, but the assurance rests on one truth: God is faithful. The believer's final holiness does not hang on the strength of human resolve but on the character of a God who finishes what He starts. He commands holiness, and He supplies it.

crossReferences.note: Rejoice in the Lord always, and pray with thanksgiving, echoing the commands here.
He who began a good work in you will carry it on to completion, matching the faithful God who will do it.
God will keep you blameless to the end, for God is faithful, who has called you into fellowship with His Son.
The God of peace equips His people to do His will, working in them what is pleasing through Jesus Christ.

study.oneStory: From the beginning God has been forming a holy people to live in His presence. As the letter ends, Paul gathers the whole hope into a prayer: that the God of peace would sanctify His people completely and keep them blameless until Christ comes. The commands paint the life of that people in the meantime, love, peace, joy, prayer, thanksgiving, and discernment, but the last word is not their effort but God's faithfulness. He who calls is faithful, and He will do it, carrying His people all the way home to the coming of the Lord Jesus.

sermon.title: All the Called Are Kept: 1 Thessalonians 5:23-28, Part 3
sermon.why: Piper works through the closing prayer to show that the sanctifying and keeping of believers rests on God's faithfulness, unfolding verse 24, He who calls you is faithful and He will do it, as the ground of the church's assurance.

quiz.q: How does Paul say the church should treat those who labor and lead among them?

options.text: Acknowledge them and hold them in the highest regard in love, because of their work.
options.note: Verses 12 and 13 call for esteem and love toward faithful leaders.

options.text: Obey them without ever weighing what they teach.
options.note: Respect is due, but Paul also calls the church to test all things.

options.text: Treat them the same as everyone else, with no special regard.
options.note: Paul urges special esteem for them because of their labor.

options.text: Suspect them and keep them at a distance.
options.note: The command is love and high regard, not suspicion.

quiz.q: What three brief commands does Paul give for a Spirit-filled life in verses 16 to 18?

options.text: Rejoice always, pray without ceasing, and give thanks in every circumstance.
options.note: Verses 16 to 18 give exactly these three, calling it God's will in Christ.

options.text: Rejoice only when things go well, and pray occasionally.
options.note: Paul commands constant joy and unceasing prayer, not occasional.

options.text: Work hard, stay quiet, and avoid other people.
options.note: These are not the commands; the marks are joy, prayer, and thanks.

options.text: Give thanks only for the good things that happen.
options.note: It is thanks in every circumstance, trusting God through all of them.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean by not quenching the Spirit?

options.text: Do not stifle the Spirit's work; instead test everything and hold fast to what is good.
options.note: Verses 19 to 21 pair not quenching the Spirit with testing and holding the good.

options.text: Accept every claimed word from anyone without question.
options.note: Paul honors the Spirit but commands testing, not uncritical acceptance.

options.text: Ignore prophecy and spiritual gifts entirely.
options.note: That would quench the Spirit; Paul says do not treat prophecies with contempt.

options.text: Suppress all emotion in worship.
options.note: The concern is the Spirit's work, not the presence or absence of emotion.

quiz.q: What does Paul pray for the church in verse 23?

options.text: That the God of peace would sanctify them completely and keep them blameless until Christ comes.
options.note: Verse 23 is a prayer for complete sanctification and preservation.

options.text: That they would make themselves perfect by their own effort.
options.note: It is God who sanctifies; the holiness is His work in them.

options.text: That they would be spared all hardship in this life.
options.note: The prayer is for holiness and preservation, not a life free of trouble.

options.text: That only their souls, not their bodies, would be saved.
options.note: Paul prays for the whole person, spirit, soul, and body.

quiz.q: On what does Paul rest the certainty that God will sanctify and keep His people?

options.text: On God's faithfulness: the One who calls you is faithful, and He will do it.
options.note: Verse 24 grounds the assurance in God's faithfulness, not human strength.

options.text: On the believers' success at keeping all the commands.
options.note: The commands matter, but the certainty rests on God, who will do it.

options.text: On the leaders being strong enough to hold the church together.
options.note: The confidence is in the faithful God, not in human leaders.

options.text: On the hope that God might change His mind and help.
options.note: God is faithful and will surely do it; it is not a hope He might act.

questions: Paul asks the church to honor and love those who labor among them. Who has worked hard for your soul, and how could you show them real regard this week?
Rejoice always, pray without ceasing, give thanks in everything. Which of these three do you find hardest right now, and why?

Paul warns against quenching the Spirit while also urging us to test all things. Where do you lean, toward stifling the Spirit or toward accepting things uncritically, and how might you grow in discerning openness?

Paul prays that God would sanctify you completely, spirit, soul, and body. What part of your life still feels far from that whole-person holiness, and how does it help to know God Himself does this work?
He who calls you is faithful, and He will do it. How does resting on God's faithfulness, rather than your own effort, change the way you pursue holiness?

translationNotes.note: The word rendered unruly is a military term for one out of rank or breaking step, describing the idle and disorderly rather than merely the rebellious.
The BSB footnote offers an alternative rendering, may your spirit, soul, and body be kept entirely blameless, keeping the sense of the whole person preserved for the day of Christ.
The BSB footnote observes that the Byzantine and Textus Receptus traditions add Amen at the end of the letter.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Thessalonians 5:12-28. All seventeen verses confirmed; none required correction. Footnotes at 5:23 and 5:28 recorded in translationNotes. No em or en dashes used.
